name: Budha
description: Budha, in Hindu mythology, is the deity associated with the planet Mercury and Wednesday. He is considered the son of Chandra (the Moon) and Tara (the wife of Brihaspati, the guru of the gods). His birth story varies, with some accounts detailing a celestial abduction and others a willing union. Budha is not to be confused with the Buddha, the founder of Buddhism. He is depicted as a handsome, mild-mannered god, often riding a chariot or a lion. Budha is revered as the god of merchandise, protector of merchants, and patron of knowledge, wisdom, and communication. He is also associated with intelligence, wit, and eloquence. He is believed to bestow these qualities upon his devotees. He governs trade, commerce, education, and writing. Budha is generally considered a benevolent deity, although his influence can sometimes be associated with trickery or instability due to his connection with the ever-changing nature of Mercury. He is often invoked for success in business, academic pursuits, and communication skills. He is worshipped for clarity of mind, good fortune, and protection from mental ailments. He is also linked to green color and the element of earth. Budha's worship is believed to mitigate the negative effects of Mercury in one's astrological chart, bringing balance and harmony to life.
subtitle: God of Mercury, Intellect, and Commerce

keyMyths.title: Birth of Budha
keyMyths.description: Budha's birth is shrouded in mystery, born from the union of Chandra and Tara. This union, whether consensual or not, led to a celestial dispute and the birth of the intelligent Budha.

keyMyths.title: Budha's Marriage
keyMyths.description: Budha is married to Ila, who is sometimes described as a male prince transformed into a woman. Their union symbolizes the balance of masculine and feminine energies.

keyMyths.title: Budha's Influence on Communication
keyMyths.description: Devotees believe that worshipping Budha enhances their communication skills and intellect. He blesses his followers with wisdom and eloquence in speech and writing.

sources.author: Vettam Mani
sources.description: A comprehensive dictionary of characters and concepts from Hindu Puranas.
sources.text: A comprehensive dictionary of characters and concepts from Hindu Puranas.

sources.author: Roshen Dalal
sources.description: A detailed guide to Hindu mythology, philosophy, and practices.
sources.text: A detailed guide to Hindu mythology, philosophy, and practices.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: God of Wisdom & Writing | Egyptian Mythology

worshipPractices.name: Puja to Budha
worshipPractices.description: Devotees offer prayers and perform rituals to Budha, especially on Wednesdays, to seek his blessings for intelligence, success in business, and clear communication. Green-colored offerings are common.

worshipPractices.name: Budha Mantra Chanting
worshipPractices.description: Reciting Budha mantras, such as 'Om Budhaya Namaha,' is believed to appease the deity and enhance one's intellectual abilities and communication skills. It is often done 108 times.

festivals.name: Budha Jayanti
festivals.timing: Varies based on the Hindu calendar
festivals.description: While primarily associated with Gautama Buddha, some Hindus also observe this day with special prayers to Budha, seeking his blessings for wisdom and knowledge. It involves temple visits and charitable acts.

festivals.name: Navagraha Puja
festivals.timing: Throughout the year, especially during astrological transits
festivals.description: As one of the Navagrahas (nine celestial bodies), Budha is worshipped during Navagraha pujas to mitigate negative astrological effects and enhance positive influences related to intellect and communication.

cultural.modernLegacy: Budha's influence persists in modern Hindu astrology and religious practices. He is invoked for success in fields requiring intellect, communication, and business acumen. His association with Mercury continues to shape astrological beliefs and practices.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Budha's birth story is complex and varies across different Hindu texts. He is primarily known as the son of Chandra, the Moon god. However, his mother is Tara, who was the wife of Brihaspati, the guru of the gods. According to one version, Chandra was captivated by Tara's beauty and abducted her. Their union resulted in the birth of Budha. Brihaspati, enraged by this, cursed Budha. Another version suggests a more consensual relationship between Chandra and Tara. Regardless of the specific narrative, Budha's birth created significant celestial turmoil, involving gods and sages. His parentage links him to both lunar and Jupiterian energies, contributing to his unique characteristics. The circumstances of his birth are often interpreted as symbolic, representing the complexities of knowledge, desire, and cosmic balance.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Budha doesn't feature in as many prominent myths as some other deities, his role as a graha (planet) in astrology makes him significant. He is often associated with merchants and is seen as the protector of commerce. Stories often highlight his intelligence and strategic thinking. One popular narrative involves his influence on individuals' intellectual capabilities. It is believed that a strong Budha in one's birth chart indicates a sharp mind, excellent communication skills, and success in fields such as writing, mathematics, and business. He is also linked to education and learning. Some stories portray him as a teacher or mentor, imparting wisdom to those who seek it. His influence is considered both benevolent and challenging, requiring individuals to balance intellect with ethics.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Budha is primarily worshipped as one of the Navagrahas, the nine celestial bodies in Hindu astrology. Devotees seek his blessings to improve their intellect, communication skills, and business prospects. Wednesday is considered his special day, and prayers are often offered on this day. Green is his associated color, and offerings of green clothes, moong beans, and green vegetables are common. Mantras dedicated to Budha are chanted to appease him and harness his positive energy. The Budha Gayatri mantra is particularly popular. Temples dedicated to the Navagrahas often feature an idol or representation of Budha. Worshippers perform pujas (rituals) and offer prayers, seeking his guidance and protection. Fasting on Wednesdays is also a common practice to honor him. The worship of Budha is deeply intertwined with astrological beliefs and practices, aiming to mitigate negative influences and enhance positive qualities associated with the planet Mercury.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Budha's legacy extends beyond mythology and into the realms of astrology, commerce, and education. His association with Mercury has made him a symbol of intellect, communication, and trade in Hindu culture. Astrologically, Budha's position in a birth chart is believed to significantly influence an individual's mental abilities, communication style, and business acumen. His influence is sought after by students, writers, orators, and business professionals. The concept of 'Budhi' (intellect) in Hindu philosophy is closely linked to Budha, emphasizing the importance of knowledge and understanding. In modern times, his influence can be seen in the continued adherence to astrological beliefs and practices, particularly concerning career choices and educational pursuits. He represents the power of the mind and the importance of clear communication in achieving success and prosperity.

powers: Granting of prosperity and success in business
Ability to bestow knowledge and understanding
shortDescription: Budha is the Hindu deity of the planet Mercury, intellect, and communication. Son of Chandra (the Moon), he governs Wednesday and is associated with eloquence and wit.
